result:
I want to write about the moment the future becomes too heavy to carry in the present, the exact millisecond when tomorrow’s shadow outweighs today’s sunlight and you feel the vertiginous tilt of time bending you forward whether or not you consent.  I have been collecting these moments the way other people collect smooth stones or ticket stubs—little artifacts of temporal slippage—because I keep believing that if I can describe one of them accurately enough I will finally understand why the years feel shorter every year, why my dreams have begun to include out-of-date technologies (fax machines, rotary phones, the sound of a dial-up modem handshake), and why, when I wake, I sometimes cannot tell whether I am thirty-eight or eight, whether the room I am in is the one I am renting now or the one I grew up in where the wallpaper was printed with cowboys and lassos that circled nothing.

The first time I felt the future’s weight I was standing in a supermarket aisle under fluorescent tubes that hummed in the key of A-flat.  I was nine.  My mother had left me guarding the cart while she searched for the right kind of rice, and I was tracing the linoleum seams with the toe of my sneaker when I noticed the expiration date on a carton of milk: 02-14-99.  It was 1992.  Seven years looked like forever in roman numerals, a movie sequel that hadn’t been storyboarded yet.  I tried to imagine the person I would be when that milk (still liquid, still white) would technically be classified as poison.  The numbers glowed like the burn mark on a time-traveler’s arm.  I felt the cart shimmy though no one had touched it, and suddenly the aisle telescoped forward into a corridor of identical cartons, each demanding that I keep aging at the mandated rate of one second per second.  I could not explain to my mother why I was crying next to the dairy case.  She offered me a Yoplait and the promise that almost everything stays good past its date if you just smell it first.  But I already knew the rule was non-negotiable: the milk and I were both going to spoil.

Now, three decades later, I measure time in battery percentages.  My phone hits 17 % and I feel the same metallic twang I felt reading that expiration date.  Low power mode is a secular advent, a tiny purple-robed announcer whispering that the ceremony of shutdown is nearer than the ceremony of reboot.  I have begun to hoard electricity the way my grandmother hoarded plastic bags, plugging in at every café table, airport gate, or borrowed bedside as though I can trickle-charge my own metabolism.  Once, in a Berlin hostel, the only available outlet was directly above a stranger’s upper bunk; I stood on a chair at 3:00 a.m., arm extended like a raccoon reaching into a bird feeder, and felt the room tilt again—same tilt as the supermarket—because I recognized myself: a middle-aged man stealing electrons from the dark so that tomorrow can arrive intact on a glass rectangle.  The future had become a document I must not let autosave fail.

I keep thinking about the phrase “late capitalism” and how the word late used to mean deceased, as in “the late Mr. Henderson,” and now it means both deceased and tardy, a grammatical pun: the system is both dying and still stuck in traffic.  Every headline is an obituary that refuses to end.  I read that venture capital is funding companies that sell drinking water extracted from the exhaust of data centers, that someone has patented a cylindrical toaster that rotates bread like a rotisserie chicken, that an AI-generated pop star with no human counterpart has signed a seven-figure record deal.  Each invention is a milk carton shoved farther down the aisle, expiration dates multiplying like fruit flies.  I picture a warehouse the size of Kansas where every product that will ever be brainstormed in an all-night Zoom call already exists in miniature, shrink-wrapped, waiting for its assigned year to slide out of the corrugated future like a tongue depressor.

The warehouse has an address: Tomorrow, Kansas, ZIP code 00000.  I mail postcards there sometimes when I cannot sleep.  Dear Tomorrow, I write, please stop sending representatives to my dreams.  Last night one of them arrived wearing a mask of my father’s face rendered in polygons.  He opened his mouth and a QR code spilled out.  I scanned it with the phone I will someday replace and it downloaded a PDF called “User Manual for the Rest of Your Life.”  All pages were blank except the last one, which contained a single line in 4-point type: Batteries not included.  I woke up with the taste of lithium on my tongue.

My father is still alive, by the way, but he has begun to speak of himself in the past perfect: “I had always liked jazz,” he says, as though the genre has been discontinued.  He forwards me emails about perpetual motion machines and tomatoes that grow in salt water.  He wants to know if I have any inside information on when the world will switch to a four-day workweek.  I tell him I’m not on the committee.  He replies with a thumbs-up emoji that looks more like a hostage signal.  Our exchanges are time-stamped artifacts neither of us archives; they scroll upward into a fog that smells faintly of nostalgia and burnt capacitors.

I read a study that claims every person alive today will have to make one million decisions about digital consent before they die: cookie banners, location tracking, personalized coupons, facial recognition at concerts.  A million.  I do the math: roughly thirty-five decisions per day if I live to eighty.  I picture a monk in a monastery of the near future, robes stitched from recycled charging cables, kneeling before a pop-up that asks, “Allow God to use your location?”  He clicks “Deny” and is immediately reincarnated as a smart refrigerator that quizzes avocados about their ripeness schedules.  Nirvana is full-stack.

Meanwhile the actual oceans are uploading themselves into the clouds one cubic centimeter at a time.  I fly cross-country and watch from my porthole as the plane’s shadow ripples across a quilt of drought-brown rectangles.  Somewhere below, an aquifer is being converted into data so that someone can back up their NFT of a cartoon ape wearing sunglasses.  The ape’s expression is the same as mine when I realize I have forgotten to turn on two-factor authentication for my mortality.  I imagine dying and immediately receiving a text: “Did you just attempt to log in to Life from an unknown device?  If yes, enter the six-digit code sent to your next of kin.”  My next of kin is busy deleting old voicemails to free up storage.

What I am trying to say is that the future used to be a place we were going to, but now it is a place that is coming to us at fiber-optic speed, a bullet train whose headlights are already illuminating our bedroom walls.  We used to pack suitcases for it—college, marriage, career, retirement—now it unpacks us overnight, removes the batteries from our smoke detectors, installs updates while we dream of rotary phones.  I wake up and my accent has changed; the city I live in has been renamed after a payment platform; my coffee maker requires a subscription.  I am not a citizen, I am a recurring revenue stream.

I keep a list of obsolete futures.  By that I mean the futures that were promised but canceled, like jetpacks, moon vacations, four-hour workdays, the end of history.  I write them on index cards and shuffle them like tarot.  Yesterday I drew “Smell-O-Vision” and realized Netflix will probably resurrect it as an optional add-on: scented candles that sync via Bluetooth, so when a rom-com couple walks through a farmers’ market your living room fills with the smell of overripe peaches and gentrification.  The card smelled faintly of 1987, the year my parents took me to Epcot and I believed the monorail was proof that cities could be elevators.  I wanted to live on the second floor of tomorrow, press a button, arrive at adulthood already helmeted for space.

Instead I became a person who googles “how to whistle loudly with two fingers” at 2:00 a.m. because I have forgotten and feel that this is the sort of skill that could save my life in an alley or an apocalypse.  The tutorial buffers.  A banner ad offers to connect me with former classmates who have also googled whistle-related queries.  I close the tab and practice in the bathroom mirror, producing a sound like a fax machine giving birth.  The future is the moment you realize no one is coming to rescue you, and even if they were, they would need your plus-one RSVP sent forty-eight hours in advance.

Let me tell you about the concert held entirely in Zoom, each performer in a separate rectangle, audience members muted but invited to physically clap in our own rooms so the sound would be asynchronous, a rainstorm of one-handed applause.  The singer’s connection glitched and her face froze mid-vowel, a digital Renaissance angel trapped in a stained-glass buffer.  We watched her eyelid twitch for thirty seconds while the chat scrolled: “We lost you,” “Still beautiful,” “Can you hear us?”  I felt the tilt again, the aisle elongating, the milk cartons marching.  The future is a cathedral built from frozen expressions, all of us perpetually almost in sync.

I try to build small, local, temporary futures to keep the big one from crushing me.  I cook a pot of beans and while they simmer I declare the next hour a pocket republic where data does not count and my attention cannot be bought.  The beans simmer, I chop onions, the republic lasts exactly twelve minutes before my phone buzzes with an alert that someone in another hemisphere has replied to my comment about USB-C hubs.  I abdicate.  The beans burn.  I eat them anyway, char tasting like the overdue notices of departed civilizations.

Sometimes I think the only undefeated human activity is forwarding.  We forward emails, memes, condolences, warnings, ourselves.  My inbox is a relay race where no one remembers the baton was originally a heartbeat.  I open a message dated 2009: “Fwd: Fwd: Fwd: You will laugh until you cry!”  I scroll through seventeen screens of indented angle brackets and arrive at a pixelated video of a cat playing a keyboard.  The cat is probably dead now, its skeleton a small xylophone buried in someone’s backyard.  Still, the forward refuses to die; it circles the globe like a Voyager golden record aimed at the loneliest aliens.  I click Reply All and type: “I am sorry for everything,” then delete it because the address list includes people whose names have become street signs in cities that no longer exist.

I want to write a children’s book called “The Future Is a Sandwich,” in which a small protagonist discovers that tomorrow is edible but only if you share it.  The illustrations would be watercolor so the colors bleed unpredictably, teaching kids that uncertainty is not a bug.  The final page would be blank except for a mirror foil so the reader sees their own eyes looking back, asking without words whether they are ready to take the first bite.  I will never write this book because I do not have children and publishers insist on companion apps with push notifications.  Still, I keep the title taped to my monitor, a Post-it note lighthouse.

There is a sound I have been trying to describe: the noise the present makes as it peels away from the future.  It is not Velcro, not ripping paper, not the kissing suction of a subway door.  It is softer, almost inside the ear, like when you swallow at high altitude and your inner ear equalizes with a faint click that is both inside and outside your skull.  I heard it clearly the day I realized my sensible low-risk retirement fund had invested in a company that 3-D prints modular apartment complexes on Mars.  The click was my portfolio re-equalizing with the cosmos.  I checked the box that said “I acknowledge the risks,” which is secular for amen.

I keep returning to the cowboys on my childhood wallpaper.  Their lassos formed perfect circles that caught nothing, not a single steer, not even the repeat pattern.  At night I used to imagine them riding off the wall and into my room, tiny plastic hooves clip-clopping across the linoleum, leaving manure the size of mouse droppings.  I wanted them to rope the monsters under my bed, but they only ever swung their loops in silence, eternal rehearsal.  Now I see them as middle managers of the past, forever branding what can no longer be sold.  Their circles are the zeroes in my bank balance, the shape of every loading icon, the mouth of every drain.  Still, there is something tender in their uselessness, the way they persist in rehearsing a purpose that was never theirs.

I want to end with a prophecy, but the only truthful oracle is the fine print.  So here is the fine print, expanded to human scale: The future will contain fewer kinds of birds.  You will remember some of their names incorrectly.  Your phone will autocorrect “mourning dove” to “morning dive,” and you will picture an alarm clock leaping off a cliff.  At least once you will walk into a room whose location pin says “home” and fail to recognize the furniture.  You will google “how to forgive yourself for not saving the world” and the top result will be a sponsored link to a meditation app that costs the same as a week of groceries.  You will subscribe anyway.  The app will crash on the day you need it most.  You will open the fridge and find a carton of milk.  Its expiration date will be today.  You will smell it, hesitate, drink it anyway.  It will taste only slightly wrong, like the idea that anything can stay unspoiled forever if kept cold enough.  You will decide it is fine.  You will decide you are still hungry.  You will look for bread to make a sandwich named after something that has not happened yet.  The bread will be frozen.  You will toast it.  While it toasts you will stare at the little red coils and remember that they glow because electricity is angry metal, a thunderstorm forced to repeat itself every morning.  You will wonder whether the same could be said of your heart.  The toaster will ding.  You will spread the future on thick, sprinkle seeds of regret, seeds of maybe.  You will take a bite.  It will be enough.

prompt: Write freely about whatever you want for 2500 words.
